ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific Kampong Fried Rice entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FAN covers fried rice as a category; no Kampong-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kampong Fried Rice or fried rice dish at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kampong Fried Rice is approximately 300 g, as typically served at a hawker stall or casual restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Goreng Kampung (Kampung Fried Rice) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Nasi Goreng Kampung as a closely analogous dish. The Filipino-labelled Kampong Fried Rice served in Singapore shares the same core preparation: day-old rice stir-fried in a wok with dried anchovies (ikan bilis), egg, shallots, garlic, chilli, and soy sauce. Values are adapted from HPB Nasi Goreng Kampung per-100 g data. Where HPB data were not available for micronutrients, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for fried rice with egg and fish was used as a secondary cross-check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kampong Fried Rice. GI of 64 is adopted from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice, stir-fried with egg and soy sauce), which is the closest analogous preparation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 79.5) / 100 ≈ 50.9, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 26.5 × 3 = 79.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and dried anchovies
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (≈1260 mg per standard 300 g serving), Kampong Fried Rice contributes more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced soy sauce are strongly advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index white rice base with moderate carbohydrate load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 64 (medium) and a glycemic load of about 50.9 per standard serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a plate and pair with non-starchy vegetables or protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from cooking oil and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium (≈1260 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (≈3.3 g per serving) warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions and requesting less oil during preparation can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kampong Fried Rice (Malay: Nasi Goreng Kampung; Chinese: 甘榜炒饭) is a Southeast Asian fried rice dish prepared by stir-frying day-old cooked white rice in a hot wok with dried anchovies (ikan bilis), egg, shallots, garlic, fresh chilli, and soy sauce. The term 'kampong' or 'kampung' means 'village' in Malay, evoking a rustic, home-style preparation. The dish is widely served across Singapore, Malaysia, and the Philippines, and is a staple at hawker centres and casual cafes. The crispy dried anchovies provide a salty, umami-rich flavour and a contrasting crunchy texture against the soft rice. The egg is typically scrambled directly into the wok, adding richness and protein. Nutritionally, a standard 300 g serving provides approximately 525 kcal, 17.4 g protein, 15.6 g fat, and 79.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is notably high at approximately 1260 mg per serving due to soy sauce and anchovies, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The medium glycemic index (approximately 64) and high glycemic load per serving require portion moderation for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is generally halal and dairy-free but contains fish, egg, and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kampong Fried Rice?
Is Kampong Fried Rice suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Kampong Fried Rice?
What are the ingredients in Kampong Fried Rice?

Can I eat Kampong Fried Rice if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo Nasi Goreng Kampung, which is the closest approved primary source for this dish as served in Singapore. The Filipino-labelled Kampong Fried Rice at Lucky Cafe shares the same core preparation profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for stir-fried white rice. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact HPB entry for 'Kampong Fried Rice' under the Filipino cuisine label was found; the adaptation from Nasi Goreng Kampung is well-justified but not an exact match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo source for this dish category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in stir-fried rice preparations per HPB data. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite for fried rice with egg as a secondary cross-check. The food is labelled 'Filipino' cuisine in the field survey source but the dish is essentially identical to Malay/Singaporean Nasi Goreng Kampung; multilingual names reflect the Singaporean hawker context in which it is served. Serving size of 300 g is based on typical hawker plate portion for fried rice in Singapore. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Kampong Fried Rice was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date.
